title: Oscar-Nominated Hamnet Production Designer Fiona Crombie on Re-Inventing Shakespeare's Home & the Globe Theater
text: Oscar-nominated production designer Fiona Crombie discusses her approach to recreating 16th-century Tudor architecture for Chloé Zhao's film adaptation of Hamnet, which centers on William Shakespeare's family coping with their son's death. Rather than precisely replicating the historic Henley Street House, Crombie prioritized capturing its atmosphere. She worked collaboratively with cinematographer Lukasz Zal to create a footprint that functioned better for filming while maintaining authentic period details like massive fireplaces, low ceilings, and winding staircases. The production imported approximately 20 tons of aged oak beams from dismantled French barns, distributed across three major sets. Crombie notes that skilled craftspeople created additional period details through hand-painted scenic work and clever material choices. Crombie's version of the Globe Theatre scales down the historic venue to 70 percent of its actual size to create intimacy between performers and audience. While the exterior and stage layout remain relatively accurate, she followed Zhao's directive to make the interior feel like the inside of a tree. For Agnes's London apartment, Crombie employed high walls that restricted the sky view, visually reflecting the character's sense of confinement within the bustling city.
